title: Genetic Cardiomyopathies
section: Disorders of the Heart Muscles Valves and Pericardium
authors:
Neal K. Lakdawala, Lynne Warner

Stevenson, Joseph Loscalzo

items.title: Prevalence and Inheritance Patterns of Genetic Cardiomyopathy
items.content: Understanding the prevalence and inheritance patterns is crucial for diagnosing genetic cardiomyopathy. A significant proportion, at least 30%, of dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM) without other clear etiology has a hereditary component. Most familial cardiomyopathies follow an autosomal dominant pattern, with some cases showing autosomal recessive, mitochondrial, or X-linked inheritance.
keyPoints: At least 30% of DCM without other clear etiology is heritable.
Most familial cardiomyopathies are inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern.
Occasional autosomal recessive, matrilineal (mitochondrial), and X-linked inheritance patterns exist.
items.reference: Genetic Cardiomyopathies · Genetic Cardiomyopathies

items.title: Role of Genetic Variants in Cardiomyopathy Pathophysiology
items.content: Missense variants with amino acid substitutions and truncating variants are common genetic abnormalities in cardiomyopathy. These can interfere with the function of normal alleles through dominant negative mechanisms or cause haploinsufficiency, contributing to disease pathophysiology.
keyPoints: Common genetic abnormalities include missense variants and truncating variants.
Dominant negative mechanisms and haploinsufficiency are key pathogenic processes.
Both mechanisms may contribute to the pathophysiology of some forms of genetic DCM.
items.reference: Genetic Cardiomyopathies · Genetic Cardiomyopathies

items.title: Clinical Implications of Genetic Testing in Cardiomyopathy
items.content: Genetic testing for cardiomyopathy primarily aids family evaluations and risk stratification. It can also identify specific therapies, such as enzyme replacements for metabolic disorders like Fabry's disease. The clinical course cannot be predicted solely based on genetic variants; thus, treatment is phenotype-based.
keyPoints: Genetic testing informs family evaluations and risk stratification.
Specific therapies may be indicated by certain genetic findings (e.g., enzyme replacement in metabolic diseases).
Treatment decisions are based on clinical phenotype rather than specific genetic variants.
items.reference: Genetic Cardiomyopathies · Genetic Cardiomyopathies

items.title: Prevalence and Impact of Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy
items.content: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) is a significant cause of sudden death in young individuals and heart failure. It affects approximately 1 in 500 people across North America, Africa, and Asia.
keyPoints: Prevalence: ~1:500
Leading cause of sudden death in the young
Important cause of heart failure
items.reference: Genetic Cardiomyopathies · EPIDEMIOLOGY, ETIOLOGY, AND PATHOLOGY

items.title: Genetic Basis and Variability in Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy
items.content: HCM is often associated with sarcomere gene variants, present in 40-50% of patients. The most common genes involved are MYH7 and MYBPC3.
keyPoints: Sarcomere gene variant prevalence: ~40–50%
Common genes: MYH7 and MYBPC3
Variants lead to different hypertrophy patterns and clinical outcomes
items.reference: Genetic Cardiomyopathies · EPIDEMIOLOGY, ETIOLOGY, AND PATHOLOGY

items.title: Pathophysiology of Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy
items.content: HCM involves enhanced calcium sensitivity and abnormal energetics due to sarcomere variants. It is characterized by nonuniform ventricular thickening, often in the interventricular septum.
keyPoints: Enhanced calcium sensitivity and ATPase activity
Misalignment and disarray of myofibrils
Nonuniform ventricular thickening, typically at the interventricular septum
items.reference: Genetic Cardiomyopathies · EPIDEMIOLOGY, ETIOLOGY, AND PATHOLOGY

items.title: Role of Cardiac Imaging in Diagnosing Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy
items.content: Cardiac imaging is crucial for diagnosing hypertrophic cardiomyopathy due to the insensitivity of physical examination and ECG. It helps differentiate between pathologic hypertrophy and physiologic changes such as those seen in athlete's heart.
keyPoints: Physical examination and ECG are insensitive for diagnosis
Biopsy not recommended, used only to exclude other diseases

Athlete's heart shows regression with cessation of training and is characterized by supernormal exercise capacity ($VO_{2max} > 50$ mL/kg per min), mild ventricular dilation, normal diastolic function
items.reference: Genetic Cardiomyopathies · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Distinguishing Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy from Athlete's Heart
items.content: Differentiating hypertrophic cardiomyopathy from athlete's heart is essential due to their overlapping features. Key distinguishing factors include regression of hypertrophy with cessation of training and specific exercise capacity metrics in athlete's heart.
keyPoints: Athlete's heart shows physiologic hypertrophy that regresses with cessation of training
$VO_{2max} > 50$ mL/kg per min indicates supernormal exercise capacity in athlete's heart
Normal diastolic function is observed in athlete's heart, unlike in hypertrophic cardiomyopathy
items.reference: Genetic Cardiomyopathies · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Management of Left Ventricular Outflow Tract Obstruction
items.content: The primary approach to managing left ventricular outflow tract obstruction in hypertrophic cardiomyopathy involves medical therapy. $\beta$-Adrenergic blocking agents and L-type calcium channel blockers, such as verapamil, are first-line treatments that reduce obstruction severity by slowing the heart rate, enhancing diastolic filling, and decreasing contractility.
keyPoints: $\beta$-Adrenergic blocking agents and L-type calcium channel blockers (e.g., verapamil) are first-line for reducing outflow tract obstruction.
Disopyramide may be added occasionally for persistent symptoms of exertional dyspnea or chest pain.
Cardiac myosin inhibitors like mavacamten have shown high efficacy in treatment, but require frequent echocardiographic monitoring due to transient reduction in LVEF.
items.reference: Genetic Cardiomyopathies · Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy

items.title: Septal Reduction Therapy Indications and Outcomes
items.content: In patients with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy who have severe, medically refractory symptoms (~5%), septal reduction therapy may be effective. This includes surgical myectomy or alcohol septal ablation. Surgical myectomy involves excising part of the septal myocardium to relieve obstruction, while alcohol septal ablation uses controlled infarction of the proximal septum.
keyPoints: Severe symptoms develop in ~5% of patients, indicating potential need for septal reduction therapy.
Surgical myectomy and alcohol septal ablation are effective for relieving outflow tract obstruction.
Neither procedure improves outcomes beyond symptom relief; complete heart block requiring permanent pacing is a common complication.
items.reference: Genetic Cardiomyopathies · Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy

items.title: Risk of Sudden Cardiac Death and Defibrillator Use
items.content: Patients with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy have an increased risk of sudden cardiac death from ventricular tachyarrhythmias. Implantable cardioverter-defibrillators (ICDs) are advised for patients with major risk factors, though the positive predictive value is low. Risk stratification may involve European Society of Cardiology risk scores and contemporary technologies like cardiac MRI.
keyPoints: Risk of sudden death from ventricular tachyarrhythmias exists in hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.
ICDs are advised for patients with major risk factors, but many never receive appropriate device therapy.
Risk stratification can include European Society of Cardiology scores and cardiac MRI.
items.reference: Genetic Cardiomyopathies · Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy

items.title: Prognosis of Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy
items.content: The prognosis for hypertrophic cardiomyopathy has improved compared to earlier studies but remains worse than in age-matched populations without the condition. The risk of sudden death is less than 1% per year, while up to 20% may progress to systolic dysfunction with an ejection fraction below 50%, potentially leading to heart failure and increased mortality unless managed by cardiac transplantation.
keyPoints: Sudden death risk <1% per year
Up to 1 in 20 patients develop systolic dysfunction (ejection fraction <50%)
Increased risk of death from progressive heart failure without timely cardiac transplantation
items.reference: Genetic Cardiomyopathies · PROGNOSIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY, ETIOLOGY, AND PATHOLOGY

items.title: Etiology and Prevalence of Dilated Cardiomyopathy (DCM)
items.content: Dilated cardiomyopathy is characterized by an enlarged left ventricle with reduced systolic function, defined as a left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) ≤0.50 or a diastolic dimension >95% predicted for age and sex. Familial clustering occurs in 30-40% of cases, with monogenic etiologies identified in about 25%. Truncating variants in the TTN gene are the most common genetic cause.
keyPoints: DCM characterized by LVEF ≤0.50 or left ventricular diastolic dimension >95%
Familial clustering present in ~30-40% of cases
Monogenic etiologies identified in ~25%
Truncating variants in TTN are the most common genetic cause
items.reference: Genetic Cardiomyopathies · PROGNOSIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY, ETIOLOGY, AND PATHOLOGY

items.title: Genetic Variants and Cardiomyopathy Phenotypes
items.content: Sarcomere variants are linked to both hypertrophic and dilated cardiomyopathies. Desmin and dystrophin defects can lead to restrictive or dilated phenotypes, respectively. Channelopathies like SCN5A variants may cause DCM with conduction disease. Nuclear membrane protein defects often result in arrhythmias and conduction system diseases, influencing sudden death risk stratification.
keyPoints: Sarcomere variants linked to hypertrophic and dilated cardiomyopathy
Desmin defects can lead to restrictive phenotype (RCM)
Dystrophin defects cause Duchenne's and Becker's muscle dystrophy with DCM
SCN5A variants implicated in DCM with conduction disease
Nuclear membrane protein defects associated with arrhythmias and sudden death risk
items.reference: Genetic Cardiomyopathies · PROGNOSIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY, ETIOLOGY, AND PATHOLOGY

items.subtopic: PROGNOSIS | CARDIOMYOPATHY DUE TO INHERITED DISORDERS OF METABOLISM | RESTRICTIVE CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.title: Genetic Influence on Prognosis and Treatment in Cardiomyopathies
items.content: The prognosis and treatment of dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM) and arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy (ACM) are influenced by the specific genetic variants present. Certain genes, such as DES, DSP, DSC2, DCG2, FLNC, LMNA, PKP2, PLN, RBM20, SCN5A, and TMEM43, are associated with a higher risk of sudden death. The progression rate is also heritable, with some variants like TTN truncating variants potentially allowing recovery with medical therapy.
keyPoints: Higher risk of sudden death in patients with specific gene variants (e.g., DES, DSP, DSC2, DCG2, FLNC, LMNA, PKP2, PLN, RBM20, SCN5A, TMEM43).
Rate of disease progression is heritable and varies by gene.
TTN truncating variants may allow recovery with medical therapy.
items.reference: Genetic Cardiomyopathies · PROGNOSIS | CARDIOMYOPATHY DUE TO INHERITED DISORDERS OF METABOLISM | RESTRICTIVE CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.subtopic: PROGNOSIS | CARDIOMYOPATHY DUE TO INHERITED DISORDERS OF METABOLISM | RESTRICTIVE CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.title: Implantable Cardioverter-Defibrillator Considerations in Cardiomyopathies
items.content: In dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM), the decision to implant a cardioverter-defibrillator may be influenced by specific genotypes. For instance, pathogenic variants such as LMNA or FLNC, or a family history of early sudden death, might warrant placement before left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) declines below 0.35.
keyPoints: Genotype plays a significant role in ICD decisions for DCM.
ICD may be warranted with pathogenic LMNA or FLNC variants or family history of early sudden death, even if LVEF > 0.35.
items.reference: Genetic Cardiomyopathies · PROGNOSIS | CARDIOMYOPATHY DUE TO INHERITED DISORDERS OF METABOLISM | RESTRICTIVE CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.subtopic: PROGNOSIS | CARDIOMYOPATHY DUE TO INHERITED DISORDERS OF METABOLISM | RESTRICTIVE CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.title: Fabry's Disease and Its Cardiac Implications
items.content: Fabry's disease, caused by alpha-galactosidase A deficiency due to GLA variants, can lead to cardiac involvement characterized by glycolipid accumulation. Diagnosis is confirmed through enzyme activity assessment or GLA sequencing. Enzyme replacement therapy, though costly (> $100,000/year), and oral chaperone therapy (migalastat) are treatment options.
keyPoints: Fabry's disease results from alpha-galactosidase A deficiency due to GLA variants.
Cardiac involvement includes glycolipid accumulation.
Diagnosis via enzyme activity assessment or GLA sequencing.
Enzyme replacement therapy is costly (> $100,000/year).
Migalastat approved for certain GLA variant carriers.
items.reference: Genetic Cardiomyopathies · PROGNOSIS | CARDIOMYOPATHY DUE TO INHERITED DISORDERS OF METABOLISM | RESTRICTIVE CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.explanation: Most familial cardiomyopathies are inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern.

options: It is too invasive and risky.

To exclude infiltrative and metabolic diseases, but not to diagnose hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.

items.statement: Sex does not influence the penetrance or clinical severity of cardiomyopathy.
